title.zh: 卷七十 傅常鄭甘陳段傳
title.en: Volume 70: Fu, Chang, Zheng, Gan, Chen and Duan
title.raw: 卷七十 傅常鄭甘陳段傳 Volume 70: Fu, Chang, Zheng, Gan, Chen and Duan

sentences.zh: 傅介子

translations.literal: Fu Jiezi (section heading).
translations.idiomatic: Fu Jiezi

sentences.zh: 傅介子，北地人也，以從軍為官。

translations.literal: Fu Jiezi was a man of Beidi; by following the army he became an official.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Jiezi came from Beidi and won office through military service.

sentences.zh: 先是，龜茲、樓蘭皆嘗殺漢使者，語在《西域傳》。

translations.literal: Before this, Qiuci and Loulan had both once killed Han envoys; the account is in the 'Treatise on the Western Regions.'
translations.idiomatic: Earlier both Qiuci and Loulan had murdered Han envoys, as told in the Treatise on the Western Regions.

sentences.zh: 至元鳳中，介子以駿馬監求使大宛，因詔令青樓蘭、龜茲國。

translations.literal: By the Yuanfeng period Jiezi, as supervisor of fine horses, sought mission to Dawan, and by edict was ordered to go to Loulan and Qiuci states.
translations.idiomatic: During the Yuanfeng years Fu Jiezi, serving as supervisor of the imperial stud, volunteered for an embassy to Ferghana and received orders to call on Loulan and Qiuci.

sentences.zh: 介子至樓蘭，責其王教匈奴遮殺漢使：「大兵方至，王苟不教匈奴，匈奴使過至諸國，何為不言？」

translations.literal: Jiezi reached Loulan and reproached its king for teaching the Xiongnu to intercept and kill Han envoys: The great army is about to arrive; if the king had not abetted the Xiongnu, when Xiongnu envoys passed through to the various states, why did you not report it?
translations.idiomatic: In Loulan he rebuked the king for letting the Xiongnu ambush and murder Han envoys: A great host is on its way; if you had not colluded with the nomads, you would have reported their couriers passing west—why did you keep silent?

sentences.zh: 王謝服，言：「匈奴使屬過，當至烏孫，道過龜茲。」

translations.literal: The king apologized and submitted, saying: Xiongnu envoys have been passing in succession; they were bound for Wusun and went by way of Qiuci.
translations.idiomatic: The king apologized and explained that Xiongnu envoys had been passing through on the road to Wusun via Qiuci.

sentences.zh: 介子至龜茲，復責其王，王亦服罪。

translations.literal: Jiezi reached Qiuci and again reproached its king; the king also admitted guilt.
translations.idiomatic: At Qiuci he repeated his charge, and that king too confessed fault.

sentences.zh: 介子從大宛還到龜茲，龜茲言：「匈奴使從烏孫還，在此。」

translations.literal: Jiezi from Dawan returned to Qiuci; Qiuci said: Xiongnu envoys returning from Wusun are here.
translations.idiomatic: On his way back from Ferghana he stopped again at Qiuci, where he was told the Xiongnu embassy returning from Wusun was still in town.

sentences.zh: 介子因率其吏士共誅斬匈奴使者。

translations.literal: Jiezi thereupon led his clerks and soldiers together to execute and behead the Xiongnu envoys.
translations.idiomatic: He then led his escort in cutting down the Xiongnu envoys.

sentences.zh: 還奏事，詔拜介子為中郎，遷平樂監。

translations.literal: Returning he reported affairs; an edict appointed Jiezi gentleman of the palace and promoted him to supervisor of Pingle.
translations.idiomatic: When he reported at court he was named a gentleman of the palace and promoted to supervisor of the Pingle lodge.

sentences.zh: 介子謂大將軍霍光曰：「樓蘭、龜茲數反覆而不誅，無所懲艾。

translations.literal: Jiezi said to Major General Huo Guang: Loulan and Qiuci repeatedly turn traitor yet are not executed; there is nothing to warn and cut them.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Jiezi told Major General Huo Guang, Loulan and Qiuci keep switching sides unpunished; nothing checks them.

sentences.zh: 介子過龜茲時，其王近就人，易得也，願往刺之，以威示諸國。」

translations.literal: When Jiezi passed Qiuci its king drew near to people and was easy to get at; I wish to go stab him, in order by awe to show the various states.
translations.idiomatic: When I passed Qiuci the king moved among his people and was within reach; let me assassinate him and overawe the western states.

sentences.zh: 大將軍曰：「龜茲道遠，且驗之於樓蘭。」

translations.literal: The major general said: Qiuci is far by road; for now test it on Loulan.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang answered, Qiuci lies too far away for now—try the lesson on Loulan first.

sentences.zh: 於是白遣之。

translations.literal: Thereupon he reported and dispatched him.
translations.idiomatic: He approved the mission and sent him off.

sentences.zh: 介子與士卒俱繼金幣，揚言以賜外國為名。

translations.literal: Jiezi with soldiers together carried gold and silk, openly declaring the pretext of bestowing gifts on foreign states.
translations.idiomatic: He and his men loaded gold and silks and gave out that they were bearing imperial gifts for the western states.

sentences.zh: 至樓蘭，樓蘭王意不親介子，介子陽引去，至其西界，使譯謂曰：「漢使者持黃金、錦繡行賜諸國，王不來受，我去之西國矣。」

translations.literal: Reaching Loulan, the Loulan king's intent was not close to Jiezi; Jiezi feigned leading away, reaching their western border, and sent an interpreter to say: The Han envoy carries gold and brocade going to bestow on the various states; if the king does not come receive them, I will leave for the western states.
translations.idiomatic: The Loulan king kept him at arm's length, so Jiezi pretended to march off to the western frontier and had his interpreter announce that the Han envoy bore gold and brocade for every kingdom—if the king would not come to take his share, the gifts would go west to others.

sentences.zh: 即出金幣以示譯。

translations.literal: Immediately he brought out gold and silk to show the interpreter.
translations.idiomatic: He produced the treasure for the interpreter to see.

sentences.zh: 譯還報王，王貪漢物，來見使者。

translations.literal: The interpreter returned and reported to the king; the king coveted Han goods and came to see the envoy.
translations.idiomatic: The interpreter carried word to the king, who could not resist the bait and rode out to meet the envoy.

sentences.zh: 介子與坐飲，陳物示之。

translations.literal: Jiezi with him sat and drank, displaying goods to show him.
translations.idiomatic: Jiezi sat him down to wine and spread the gifts before him.

sentences.zh: 飲酒皆醉，介子謂王曰：「天子使我私報王。」

translations.literal: When drinking all were drunk, Jiezi said to the king: The Son of Heaven sent me to report privately to the king.
translations.idiomatic: When both were drunk Jiezi whispered that the emperor had a confidential message for him alone.

sentences.zh: 王起隨介子入帳中，屏語，壯士二人從後刺之，刃交胸，立死。

translations.literal: The king rose and followed Jiezi into the tent; with screen of speech, two stalwarts from behind stabbed him, blades crossing his breast; he died at once.
translations.idiomatic: The king followed him into the tent for a private word; two braves slipped in behind and drove their blades through his chest so that he dropped dead.

sentences.zh: 其貴人左右皆散走。

translations.literal: His nobles and attendants all scattered and fled.
translations.idiomatic: His nobles and bodyguards fled in panic.

sentences.zh: 介子告諭以：「王負漢罪，天子遣我業誅王，當更立前太子質在漢者。

translations.literal: Jiezi announced and instructed them: The king bore guilt toward the Han; the Son of Heaven dispatched me already to execute the king; we ought to set up anew the former heir apparent who is hostage in Han.
translations.idiomatic: Jiezi proclaimed that the king had forfeited Han favor, that the emperor had sent him to execute him, and that the crown prince then held hostage at Chang'an would be enthroned.

sentences.zh: 漢兵方至，毋敢動，動，滅國矣！」

translations.literal: Han troops are about to arrive; do not dare move; if you move, the state will be extinguished!
translations.idiomatic: A Han army is on the march—stir and your kingdom is erased!

sentences.zh: 遂持王首還詣闕，公卿將軍議者咸嘉其功。

translations.literal: Then carrying the king's head he returned to the palace gate; ministers, generals, and deliberators all praised his merit.
translations.idiomatic: He brought the king's head to the capital, where every minister and general praised the deed.

sentences.zh: 上乃下詔曰：「樓蘭王安歸嘗為匈奴間，候遮漢使者，發兵殺略衛司馬安樂、光祿大夫忠、期門郎遂成等三輩，及安息、大宛使，盜取節印、獻物，甚逆天理。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon issued an edict saying: Loulan king Angui once acted as Xiongnu spy, watching and intercepting Han envoys, raising troops to kill and plunder Guard Commandant Anle, Grand Counselor Zhong, gentleman-at-the-palace gate Suicheng and others in three parties, and Parthian and Dawan envoys, stealing tally-seals and tribute goods—greatly opposing heaven's principle.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then promulgated an edict: Loulan's King Angui had spied for the Xiongnu, waylaid Han envoys, sent troops who slew and plundered Commandant Anle, Grand Counselor Zhong, Gentleman Suicheng of the palace guard, and others in three separate parties, murdered Parthian and Ferghana embassies, and stole their tallies, seals, and tribute—crimes that outrage Heaven.

sentences.zh: 平樂監傅介子持節使誅斬樓蘭王安歸首，縣之北闕，以直報怨，不煩師從。

translations.literal: Supervisor of Pingle Fu Jiezi bearing tally as envoy executed and beheaded Loulan king Angui's head, suspended it at the north gate, with straightness repaying resentment, not troubling an army to follow.
translations.idiomatic: Supervisor Fu Jiezi, tally in hand, struck off Angui's head and hung it on the north gate of the palace, settling the score without mobilizing an army.

sentences.zh: 其封介子為義陽侯，食邑七百戶。

translations.literal: Let Jiezi be enfeoffed as marquis of Yiyang with revenue of seven hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: He was therefore enfeoffed as Marquis of Yiyang with seven hundred taxable households.

sentences.zh: 士刺王者皆補侍郎。」

translations.literal: Soldiers who stabbed the king shall all be appointed gentleman consultants.
translations.idiomatic: Every soldier who struck the blow was appointed gentleman consultant.

sentences.zh: 介子薨，子敞有罪不得嗣，國除。

translations.literal: Jiezi died; son Chang had crime and could not inherit; the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: When Jiezi died his son Chang was convicted and could not inherit, so the marquisate lapsed.

sentences.zh: 元始中，繼功臣世，復封介子曾孫長為義陽侯，王莽敗，乃絕。

translations.literal: In the Yuanshi period, continuing meritorious ministers' generations, again enfeoffed Jiezi's great-grandson Chang as marquis of Yiyang; when Wang Mang fell, then it ended.
translations.idiomatic: During Yuanshi the court renewed meritorious lines and re-enfeoffed his great-grandson Chang as Marquis of Yiyang until Wang Mang's fall ended the line.

sentences.zh: 常惠，太原人也。

translations.literal: Chang Hui was a man of Taiyuan.
translations.idiomatic: Chang Hui came from Taiyuan.

sentences.zh: 少時家貧，自奮應募，隨移中監蘇武使匈奴，並見拘留十餘年，昭帝時乃還。

translations.literal: In youth his family was poor; he roused himself to answer recruitment, following Supervisor of Yizhong Su Wu as envoy to the Xiongnu, together being detained over ten years; in Emperor Zhao's time then returned.
translations.idiomatic: Poor in youth, he enlisted and followed Su Wu, supervisor of the Yizhong lodge, on embassy to the Xiongnu; both were held more than ten years until they returned under Emperor Zhao.

sentences.zh: 漢嘉其勤勞，拜為光祿大夫。

translations.literal: The Han praised his toil and appointed him grand counselor of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: The court honored his endurance with appointment as grand counselor of the palace.

sentences.zh: 是時，烏孫公主上書言：「匈奴發騎田車師，車師與匈奴為一，共侵烏孫，唯天子救之！」

translations.literal: At this time the Wusun princess submitted a memorial saying: The Xiongnu have sent cavalry to plough-field Jushi; Jushi and the Xiongnu are one, jointly invading Wusun—only the Son of Heaven can save us!
translations.idiomatic: The Han princess at Wusun then memorialized: The Xiongnu have sent horsemen to raid Jushi; Jushi has gone over to them and together they strike Wusun—only Your Majesty can rescue us!

sentences.zh: 漢養士馬，議欲擊匈奴。

translations.literal: The Han raised soldiers and horses, deliberating wishing to strike the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: The Han fed troops and mounts and debated a strike against the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 會昭帝崩，宣帝初即位，本始二年，遣惠使烏孫。

translations.literal: It happened Emperor Zhao died; Emperor Xuan had just ascended; in the second year of Benshi he dispatched Hui as envoy to Wusun.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhao died before the campaign opened; in the second year of Benshi, early in Emperor Xuan's reign, the court sent Chang Hui to Wusun.

sentences.zh: 公主及昆彌皆遣使，因惠言：「匈奴連發大兵擊烏孫，取車延、惡師地，收其人民去，使使脅求公主，欲隔絕漢。

translations.literal: The princess and the kunmi both sent envoys, through Hui saying: The Xiongnu repeatedly sent great troops to strike Wusun, taking Cheyan and Wushi lands, gathering their people and carrying them off, sending envoys coercively demanding the princess, wishing to cut off the Han.
translations.idiomatic: The princess and the kunmi sent envoys with Hui to report that the Xiongnu had repeatedly invaded Wusun, seized the districts of Cheyan and Wushi, carried off the population, and demanded the princess by threat, hoping to sever Wusun from the Han.

sentences.zh: 昆彌願發國半精兵，自給人馬五萬騎，盡力擊匈奴。

translations.literal: The kunmi wishes to dispatch half the state's elite troops, himself supplying men and horses fifty thousand riders, exerting full strength to strike the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: The kunmi offers to raise half his kingdom's picked warriors—fifty thousand horsemen at his own expense—and throw them fully against the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 唯天子出兵以救公主、昆彌！」

translations.literal: Only let the Son of Heaven send troops to save the princess and the kunmi!
translations.idiomatic: He begs the emperor to march an army to save the princess and himself.

sentences.zh: 於是漢大發十五萬騎，五將軍分道出，語在《匈奴傳》。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Han greatly mobilized one hundred fifty thousand riders, five generals by separate roads going out; the account is in the 'Treatise on the Xiongnu.'
translations.idiomatic: The Han then fielded a hundred fifty thousand cavalry in five columns under five generals, as recorded in the Treatise on the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 以惠為校尉，持節護烏孫兵。

translations.literal: Hui was made colonel, bearing tally to protect Wusun troops.
translations.idiomatic: Chang Hui was named colonel with tally and staff to supervise the Wusun contingent.

sentences.zh: 昆彌自將翕侯以下五萬餘騎，從西方入至右谷蠡庭，獲單于父行及嫂居次，名王騎將以下三萬九千人，得馬、牛、驢、騾、橐佗五萬餘匹，羊六十餘萬頭，烏孫皆自取鹵獲。

translations.literal: The kunmi personally led over fifty thousand riders from the She marquis down, entering from the west to the Right Guli's court, capturing the chanyu's father's generation and sister-in-law rank, famous kings and cavalry generals down thirty-nine thousand persons, obtaining horses, cattle, donkeys, mules, camels over fifty thousand head, sheep over six hundred thousand head; Wusun all took for themselves the booty.
translations.idiomatic: The kunmi led more than fifty thousand horsemen from the Shehou rank downward, swung in from the west to the court of the Right Guli-lu, and took the chanyu's kinsmen by marriage, a princess of rank, thirty-nine thousand nobles and officers, fifty thousand odd head of horses, cattle, donkeys, mules, and camels, and six hundred thousand sheep—all of which the Wusun kept as their own spoils.

sentences.zh: 惠從吏卒十餘人隨昆彌還，未至烏孫，烏孫人盜惠印綬節。

translations.literal: Hui followed with over ten clerks and soldiers accompanying the kunmi back; before reaching Wusun, Wusun people stole Hui's seal-ribbon and tally.
translations.idiomatic: Chang Hui rode back toward Wusun with a dozen escorts, but before they arrived Wusun men stole his seal, ribbons, and staff of office.

sentences.zh: 惠還，自以當誅。

translations.literal: Hui returned, himself thinking he ought to be executed.
translations.idiomatic: He expected execution when he reached court.

sentences.zh: 時，漢五將皆無功，天子以惠奉使克獲，遂封惠為長羅侯。

translations.literal: At the time the Han five generals all had no merit; the Son of Heaven because Hui on mission achieved capture, thereupon enfeoffed Hui as marquis of Changluo.
translations.idiomatic: Because the five Han columns had won no success while his embassy had brought real gain, the emperor enfeoffed him as Marquis of Changluo.

sentences.zh: 復遣惠持金幣還賜烏孫貴人有功者，惠因奏請龜茲國嘗殺校尉賴丹，未伏誅，請便道擊之，宣帝不許。

translations.literal: Again dispatching Hui bearing gold and silk to return and bestow on Wusun nobles who had merit, Hui thereby memorialized requesting Qiuci state had once killed Colonel Lai Dan, not yet submitted to execution, requesting by convenient road to strike it; Emperor Xuan did not permit.
translations.idiomatic: He was sent back with gold and silk to reward meritorious Wusun nobles; on the way he memorialized that Qiuci had murdered Colonel Lai Dan without paying for it and asked leave to attack by detour, but Emperor Xuan refused.

sentences.zh: 大將軍霍光風惠以便宜從事。

translations.literal: Major General Huo Guang hinted to Hui to act at discretionary convenience.
translations.idiomatic: Major General Huo Guang quietly told him to use his own judgment.

sentences.zh: 惠與吏士五百人俱至烏孫，還過，發西國兵二萬人，令副使發龜茲東國二萬人，烏孫兵七千人，從三面攻龜茲，兵未合，先遣人責其王以前殺漢使狀。

translations.literal: Hui with five hundred clerks and soldiers together reached Wusun; on return passing, he mobilized western states' troops twenty thousand men, ordered the deputy envoy to mobilize Qiuci eastern states twenty thousand men, Wusun troops seven thousand men, from three sides attacking Qiuci; before troops joined, first sent men reproaching its king on the former killing Han envoy circumstances.
translations.idiomatic: Chang Hui reached Wusun with five hundred men, then on the return leg raised twenty thousand western auxiliaries, had his deputy raise twenty thousand from states east of Qiuci, and added seven thousand Wusun horse to close on Qiuci from three sides; before the columns met he sent envoys to charge the king with the old murder of Han officers.

sentences.zh: 王謝曰：「乃我先王時為貴人姑翼所誤耳，我無罪。」

translations.literal: The king apologized saying: It was in my former king's time, mistaken by noble Guyi; I have no guilt.
translations.idiomatic: The king apologized: Under my late father the noble Guyi misled the court; I am innocent.

sentences.zh: 惠曰：「即如此，縛姑翼來，吾置王。」

translations.literal: Hui said: If thus, bind Guyi and bring him; I will spare the king.
translations.idiomatic: Chang Hui replied, Then bind Guyi and deliver him, and I will spare you.

sentences.zh: 王執姑翼詣惠，惠斬之而還。

translations.literal: The king seized Guyi and brought him to Hui; Hui beheaded him and returned.
translations.idiomatic: The king handed Guyi over; Chang Hui executed him and withdrew.

sentences.zh: 後代蘇武為典屬國，明習外國事，勤勞數有功。

translations.literal: Later he replaced Su Wu as director of dependent states, clearly practiced in foreign affairs, toiling repeatedly with merit.
translations.idiomatic: He succeeded Su Wu as director of dependent states, knew frontier business thoroughly, and won repeated distinction for his labors.

sentences.zh: 甘露中，後將軍趙充國薨，天子遂以惠為右將軍，典屬國如故。

translations.literal: In the Ganlu period Rear General Zhao Chongguo died; the Son of Heaven thereupon made Hui right general, director of dependent states as before.
translations.idiomatic: During the Ganlu years, when Rear General Zhao Chongguo died, the emperor named Chang Hui right general while he kept the directorship of dependent states.

sentences.zh: 宣帝崩，惠事元帝，三歲薨，謚曰壯武侯。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan died; Hui served Emperor Yuan; after three years he died; posthumous title said stalwart martial marquis.
translations.idiomatic: He served Emperor Yuan after Emperor Xuan's death, died three years later, and was posthumously titled Stalwart Martial Marquis.

sentences.zh: 傳國至曾孫，建武中乃絕。

translations.literal: The state passed to great-grandson; in the Jianwu period then ended.
translations.idiomatic: The marquisate lasted to his great-grandson and lapsed in the Jianwu era.

sentences.zh: 鄭吉，會稽人也，以卒伍從軍，數出西域，由是為郎。

translations.literal: Zheng Ji was a man of Kuaiji; by common soldier following the army, repeatedly went out to the Western Regions, thereby becoming a gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Ji of Kuaiji rose from the ranks, campaigned repeatedly in the Western Regions, and was promoted to gentleman.

sentences.zh: 吉為人強執，習外國事。

translations.literal: Ji in person was strong and stubborn, practiced in foreign affairs.
translations.idiomatic: He was stubborn by nature and thoroughly versed in frontier affairs.

sentences.zh: 自張騫通西域，李廣利征伐之後，初置校尉，屯田渠黎。

translations.literal: From Zhang Qian opening the Western Regions, after Li Guangli's campaigns, at first a colonel was established, garrison-field farming at Quli.
translations.idiomatic: After Zhang Qian opened the west and Li Guangli's expeditions, the Han first posted a colonel to farm the garrison at Quli.

sentences.zh: 至宣帝時，吉以侍郎田渠黎，積穀，因發諸國兵攻破車師，遷衛司馬，使護鄯善以西南道。

translations.literal: By Emperor Xuan's time Ji as gentleman consultant farmed at Quli, accumulated grain, thereby mobilizing various states' troops to break Jushi, was promoted guard commandant, sent to protect Shanshan and the road southwest.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xuan he served as gentleman consultant on the Quli farms, built up grain stores, raised allied troops to crush Jushi, was promoted guard commandant, and was charged with the road southwest of Shanshan.

sentences.zh: 神爵中，匈奴乖亂，日逐王先賢撣欲降漢，使人與吉相聞。

translations.literal: In the Shenjue period the Xiongnu were rebellious and chaotic; the Rizhi king Xianxianshan wished to surrender to the Han and sent a man to communicate with Ji.
translations.idiomatic: During Shenjue the Xiongnu court split; the Rizhi king Xianxianshan meant to defect and sent envoys to Zheng Ji.

sentences.zh: 吉發渠黎、龜茲諸國五萬人迎日逐王，口萬二千人、小王將十二人隨吉至河曲，頗有亡者，吉追斬之，遂將詣京師。

translations.literal: Ji mobilized fifty thousand men from Quli, Qiuci, and various states to welcome the Rizhi king; twelve thousand persons by count and twelve petty-king generals followed Ji to the river bend; there were quite deserters; Ji pursued and beheaded them, then led them to the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Ji raised fifty thousand men from Quli, Qiuci, and neighboring states to escort the Rizhi king; twelve thousand tribesmen and twelve petty kings followed him to the great bend of the Yellow River, but some tried to bolt and he hunted them down and beheaded them before marching the whole party to Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 漢封日逐王為歸德侯。

translations.literal: The Han enfeoffed the Rizhi king as marquis of Guide.
translations.idiomatic: The Han enfeoffed the Rizhi king as Marquis of Guide.

sentences.zh: 吉既破車師，降日逐，威震西域，遂並護車師以西北道，故號都護。

translations.literal: Ji having broken Jushi and surrendered the Rizhi, his awe shook the Western Regions; thereupon he jointly protected Jushi and the northwest road, therefore titled protector-general.
translations.idiomatic: After he broke Jushi and brought in the Rizhi king his name shook the Tarim basin, and he was given charge of both Jushi and the northwestern trunk road—the office called protector-general.

sentences.zh: 都護之置自吉始焉。

translations.literal: The establishment of protector-general began with Ji.
translations.idiomatic: The protector-generalship began with Zheng Ji.

sentences.zh: 上嘉其功效，乃下詔曰：「都護西域騎都尉鄭吉，拊循外蠻，宣明威信，迎匈奴單于從兄日逐王眾，擊破車師兜訾城，功效茂著。

translations.literal: The emperor praised his merit and achievement, then issued an edict saying: Protector-general of the Western Regions Cavalry Colonel Zheng Ji has soothed outer barbarians, proclaimed clear awe and trust, welcomed the Xiongnu chanyu's cousin the Rizhi king's host, smashed Jushi's Douzi city—merit and achievement luxuriantly manifest.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor praised his service and issued an edict: Protector-general and cavalry colonel Zheng Ji has pacified the outer tribes, spread Han authority, escorted the Rizhi king—the chanyu's cousin—with his following, and stormed the Jushi stronghold of Douzi; his achievements are outstanding.

sentences.zh: 其封吉為安遠侯，食邑千戶。」

translations.literal: Let Ji be enfeoffed as marquis of Anyuan with revenue of one thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: He is therefore enfeoffed as Marquis of Anyuan with one thousand households.

sentences.zh: 吉於是中西或則立莫府，治烏壘城，鎮撫諸國，誅伐懷集之。

translations.literal: Ji thereupon in the central and western regions thereupon established a headquarters, administering Wulei city, pacifying and soothing the various states, punishing, attacking, cherishing, and gathering them.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Ji then set up his headquarters between the center and the west, governed from Wulei, and kept the states in order by force, favor, and conciliation.

sentences.zh: 漢之號令班西域矣，始自張騫而成於鄭吉。

translations.literal: Han commands were promulgated through the Western Regions; they began with Zhang Qian and were completed with Zheng Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Han decrees now ran the length of the Western Regions, a work begun by Zhang Qian and brought to fruition by Zheng Ji.

sentences.zh: 語在《西域傳》。

translations.literal: The words are in the 'Treatise on the Western Regions.'
translations.idiomatic: The details are given in the Treatise on the Western Regions.

sentences.zh: 吉薨，謚曰繆侯。

translations.literal: Ji died; posthumous title said erroneous marquis.
translations.idiomatic: When Zheng Ji died he received the posthumous title Marquis Erroneous.

sentences.zh: 子光嗣，薨，無子，國除。

translations.literal: Son Guang inherited; died; no son; state abolished.
translations.idiomatic: His son Guang inherited but died without an heir and the marquisate lapsed.

sentences.zh: 元始中，錄功臣不以罪絕者，封吉曾孫永為安遠侯。

translations.literal: In the Yuanshi period, recording meritorious ministers not cut off by crime, enfeoffed Ji's great-grandson Yong as marquis of Anyuan.
translations.idiomatic: During Yuanshi the court re-listed meritorious houses not extinguished by crime and enfeoffed his great-grandson Yong as Marquis of Anyuan.

sentences.zh: 甘延壽

translations.literal: Gan Yanshou (section heading).
translations.idiomatic: Gan Yanshou

sentences.zh: 先是，宣帝時匈奴乖亂，五單于爭立，呼韓邪單于與郅支單于俱遣子入侍，漢兩受之。

translations.literal: Before this, in Emperor Xuan's time the Xiongnu were rebellious and chaotic; five chanyus contended for the throne; Chanyu Hu Hanxie and Chanyu Zhizhi both sent sons to attend court, and the Han accepted both.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, under Emperor Xuan, the Xiongnu split into five rival chanyus; both Hu Hanxie and Zhizhi sent hostages to Chang'an, and the Han court accepted both.

sentences.zh: 後呼韓邪單于身入稱臣朝見，郅支以為呼韓邪破弱降漢，不能自還，即西收右地。

translations.literal: Later Chanyu Hu Hanxie personally entered calling himself subject and attending audience; Zhizhi thought Hu Hanxie broken weak and surrendered to Han, unable to return himself, then west gathered the right territory.
translations.idiomatic: When Hu Hanxie came in person to acknowledge Han overlordship, Zhizhi assumed he was too shattered ever to return north and seized the western steppe for himself.

sentences.zh: 會漢發兵送呼韓邪單于，郅於由是遂西破呼偈、堅昆、丁令，兼三國而都之。

translations.literal: It happened the Han raised troops to escort Chanyu Hu Hanxie; Zhizhi thereupon west broke Hujie, Jiankun, and Dingling, annexing three states and making his capital there.
translations.idiomatic: While the Han marched an escort for Hu Hanxie, Zhizhi swept west, crushed the Hujie, Jiankun, and Dingling peoples, annexed their lands, and set up his court among them.

sentences.zh: 怨漢擁護呼韓邪而不助己，困辱漢使者漢乃始等。

translations.literal: Resenting the Han for supporting Hu Hanxie and not aiding himself, he trapped and humiliated Han envoys Han Naishi and others.
translations.idiomatic: Furious that the Han backed Hu Hanxie instead of him, he seized and humiliated Han envoys who crossed his path.

sentences.zh: 初元四年，遣使奉獻，因求侍子，願為內附。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Chuyuan he sent envoys bearing tribute, thereby requesting the hostage son, wishing to become inner attached.
translations.idiomatic: In 5 BCE he sent tribute and asked for the return of his son who was held hostage, offering to submit as an inner vassal.

sentences.zh: 漢議遣衛司馬谷吉送之。

translations.literal: The Han deliberated dispatching Guard Commandant Gu Ji to escort him.
translations.idiomatic: The court debated sending Guard Commandant Gu Ji to escort the prince home.

sentences.zh: 御史大夫貢禹、博士匡衡以為《春秋》之義「許夷狄者不一而足」，今郅支單于鄉化未醇，所在絕遠，宜令使者送其子至塞而還。

translations.literal: Imperial Counselor Gong Yu and Academician Kuang Heng thought that in the Spring and Autumn's meaning 'granting barbarians need not be complete in one step'; now Chanyu Zhizhi's turning toward civilization is not yet pure; the place is extremely far; it is fitting to order the envoy escort his son to the border and return.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial Counselor Gong Yu and Academician Kuang Heng cited the Spring and Autumn maxim that concessions to barbarians may be granted in stages: Zhizhi's conversion is still shallow and his camp lies far away, so the envoy should hand over the prince at the frontier and come home.

sentences.zh: 吉上書言：「中國與夷狄有羈縻不絕之義，今既養全其子十年，德澤甚厚，空絕而不送，近從塞還，示棄捐不畜，使無鄉從之心，棄前恩，立後怨，不便。

translations.literal: Ji submitted a memorial saying: China and the barbarians have the duty of halter-rein unbroken; now having reared and kept his son ten years, favor and grace are very thick; to cut off emptily and not deliver, having him return only from near the border, shows casting away and not cherishing, causing him to lack a mind to turn toward us; casting aside former kindness, establishing later resentment—is not convenient.
translations.idiomatic: Gu Ji memorialized: The Han owe the steppe peoples the courtesy of an unbroken bridle-and-rein; we have kept Zhizhi's son at court for ten years and shown him great kindness. To stop short at the passes would look like abandonment, kill any wish to submit, throw away old favor, and breed fresh hatred—unwise in every way.

sentences.zh: 議者見前江乃始無應敵之數，知勇俱困，以致恥辱，即豫為臣憂。

translations.literal: The debaters seeing former Jiang Naishi had no tactics for meeting the enemy, knowledge and courage both trapped, thereby incurring shame, then beforehand worried for your servant.
translations.idiomatic: Critics recall how Jiang Naishi lacked a plan, wit and courage failed together, and the court suffered disgrace; they therefore fret over your servant in advance.

sentences.zh: 臣幸得建強漢之節，承明聖之詔，宣諭厚恩，不宜敢桀。

translations.literal: Your servant fortunately obtained to bear the strong Han tally, receive the clear sage's edict, proclaim and instruct thick grace; he ought not dare be violent.
translations.idiomatic: Your servant bears the tallies of a mighty Han and a sage emperor's charge to proclaim our bounty; he cannot lawfully play the bully.

sentences.zh: 若懷禽獸，加無道於臣，則單于長嬰大罪，必遁逃遠捨，不敢近邊。

translations.literal: If harboring beast mind he adds lack of way toward your servant, then the chanyu long bears great guilt, must flee far and abandon, not daring to approach the border.
translations.idiomatic: If he proves a beast and wrongs your servant, the chanyu will shoulder a grave crime and flee deep into the steppe, never again daring to approach the frontier.

sentences.zh: 沒一使以安百姓，國之計，臣之願也。

translations.literal: Losing one envoy in order to secure the hundred surnames is the state's plan and your servant's wish.
translations.idiomatic: To lose one envoy and secure the realm is sound policy and your servant's earnest wish.

sentences.zh: 願送至庭。」

translations.literal: I beg to escort him to the court.
translations.idiomatic: Your servant asks leave to deliver the prince to his father's yurt.

sentences.zh: 上以示朝者，禹復爭，以為吉往必為國取悔生事，不可許。

translations.literal: The emperor showed it to those at court; Yu again disputed, thinking if Ji goes he will surely for the state fetch regret and raise affairs; cannot permit.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor circulated the memorial; Gong Yu renewed his objection that Gu Ji's journey would bring shame and fresh trouble on the state and must be refused.

sentences.zh: 右將軍馮奉世以為可遣，上許焉。

translations.literal: Right General Feng Fengshi thought it could be dispatched; the emperor permitted.
translations.idiomatic: Right General Feng Fengshi argued for the mission, and the emperor approved.

sentences.zh: 既至，郅支單于怒，竟殺吉等。

translations.literal: When they arrived, Chanyu Zhizhi was angry and in the end killed Ji and the others.
translations.idiomatic: When they arrived Chanyu Zhizhi flew into a rage and murdered Gu Ji and his party.

sentences.zh: 自知負漢，又聞呼韓邪益強，遂西奔康居。

translations.literal: Knowing himself guilty toward the Han, and hearing Hu Hanxie grew stronger, then he fled west to Kangju.
translations.idiomatic: Knowing he had wronged the Han and that Hu Hanxie was growing stronger, he bolted west to Kangju.

sentences.zh: 康居王以女妻郅支，郅支亦以女予康居王。

translations.literal: The Kangju king gave a daughter in marriage to Zhizhi; Zhizhi also gave a daughter to the Kangju king.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Kangju married him a daughter, and Zhizhi gave a daughter to the king of Kangju in return.

sentences.zh: 康居甚尊敬郅支，欲倚其威以脅諸國。

translations.literal: Kangju greatly honored Zhizhi, wishing to rely on his awe to coerce the various states.
translations.idiomatic: Kangju courted him eagerly, hoping to lean on his prestige to cow the neighboring kingdoms.

sentences.zh: 郅支數借兵擊烏孫，深入至赤谷城，殺略民人，驅畜產，烏孫不敢追，西邊空虛，不居者且千里。

translations.literal: Zhizhi repeatedly borrowed troops to strike Wusun, deeply entering to Red Valley city, killing and plundering the people, driving off livestock; Wusun did not dare pursue; the western border was empty and hollow; unsettled reached nearly a thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: Zhizhi repeatedly borrowed Kangju troops against Wusun, drove deep to Chigu, slaughtered and plundered, and drove off herds while the Wusun dared not pursue; for nearly a thousand li the western marches lay empty.

sentences.zh: 郅支單于自以大國，威名尊重，又乘勝驕，不為康居王禮，怒殺康居王女及貴人、人民數百，或支解投都賴水中。

translations.literal: Chanyu Zhizhi himself thought great state, awe and name honored and weighty, moreover riding victory arrogant, did not perform rites to the Kangju king; in anger killed the Kangju king's daughter and nobles and several hundred people, some dismembered and cast into the Talas water.
translations.idiomatic: Zhizhi considered himself master of a great power, grew arrogant on victory, refused courtesy to the king of Kangju, and in fury murdered the king's daughter, notables, and hundreds of commoners, some of whom he had torn limb from limb and thrown into the Talas River.

sentences.zh: 發民作城，日作五百人，二歲乃已。

translations.literal: He mobilized the people to build a wall, daily working five hundred men; two years then finished.
translations.idiomatic: He drafted the people to build a fortress, five hundred laborers a day, for two years before it was done.

sentences.zh: 又遣使責闔蘇、大宛諸國歲遺，不敢不予。

translations.literal: Again he sent envoys to demand from Hesu, Dawan, and various states yearly tribute; they did not dare not give.
translations.idiomatic: He also sent envoys to demand annual tribute from Hesu, Ferghana, and other states, and none dared refuse.

sentences.zh: 漢遣使三輩至康居求谷吉等死，郅支困辱使者，不肯奉詔，而因都護上書言：「居困厄，願歸計強漢，遣子入侍。」

translations.literal: The Han sent envoys in three parties to Kangju to demand an accounting for Gu Ji and others' deaths; Zhizhi trapped and humiliated the envoys, refused to obey edicts, yet through the protector-general submitted a memorial saying: I dwell in hardship; I wish to submit plans to the strong Han and send a son to attend court.
translations.idiomatic: The Han sent three missions to Kangju demanding justice for Gu Ji's murder; Zhizhi abused every envoy, ignored imperial decrees, yet relayed through the protector-general a memorial pleading hardship, professing wish to submit to the mighty Han, and offering to send a son as hostage.

sentences.zh: 其驕嫚如此。

translations.literal: His arrogance and slowness were thus.
translations.idiomatic: Such was his arrogance.

sentences.zh: 建昭三年，湯與延壽出西域。

translations.literal: In the third year of Jianzhao, Tang and Yanshou went out to the Western Regions.
translations.idiomatic: In 36 BCE Chen Tang and Gan Yanshou marched into the Western Regions.

sentences.zh: 湯為人沉勇有大慮，多策謀，喜奇功，每過城邑山川，常登望。

translations.literal: Tang in person was deep and brave with great plans, many stratagems, fond of strange merit; whenever passing cities, towns, mountains, and rivers, he always climbed to look out.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Tang was steady and bold, full of long-range schemes and stratagems, eager for striking success, and whenever his route crossed a town or height he would climb to survey the ground.

sentences.zh: 既領外國，與延壽謀曰：「夷狄畏服大種，其天性也。

translations.literal: Having taken charge of foreign regions, he plotted with Yanshou, saying: Barbarians fear and submit to a great tribe; it is their heaven-nature.
translations.idiomatic: Once he held frontier command he said to Gan Yanshou, Steppe peoples bow to the strongest power—that is their nature.

sentences.zh: 西域本屬匈奴，今郅支單于威名遠聞，侵陵烏孫、大宛，常為康居畫計，欲降服之。

translations.literal: The Western Regions originally belonged to the Xiongnu; now Chanyu Zhizhi's awe and name are heard far; he invades and oppresses Wusun and Dawan, constantly for Kangju draws plans, wishing to subdue and take them.
translations.idiomatic: The west once answered to the Xiongnu; now Zhizhi's fame carries far while he bullies Wusun and Ferghana and plots with Kangju to bring them to heel.

sentences.zh: 如得此二國，北擊伊列，西取安息，南排月氏、山離烏弋，數年之間，城郭諸國危矣。

translations.literal: If he obtains these two states, north he strikes Yilie, west takes Anxi, south pushes aside Yuezhi, Shanli, and Wuyi; within several years the walled states will be in peril.
translations.idiomatic: If he wins those two he can strike north toward Ili, west toward Parthia, and south against the Yuezhi and the Shanli-Wuyi belt; within a few years every oasis kingdom will be in danger.

sentences.zh: 且其人剽悍，好戰伐，數取勝，久畜之，必為西域患。

translations.literal: Moreover his people are fierce and hardy, fond of war and attack, repeatedly gaining victory; long nourished, they will surely become a Western Regions peril.
translations.idiomatic: His warriors are fierce, war-loving, and used to winning; let them grow strong and they will become a scourge to the whole Tarim basin.

sentences.zh: 郅支單于雖所在絕遠，蠻夷無金城強弩之守，如發屯田吏士，驅從烏孫眾兵，直指其城下，彼亡則無所之，守則不足自保，千載之功可一朝而成也。」

translations.literal: Although Chanyu Zhizhi dwells in extreme distance, barbarians lack metal walls and strong crossbows' defense; if we mobilize garrison-field clerks and soldiers, drive and follow Wusun's mass troops, point straight to below his walls—if he flees he has nowhere to go; if he guards he is insufficient to preserve himself—a thousand ages' merit can be completed in one morning.
translations.idiomatic: Zhizhi lies far off, but nomads have no ringed cities or heavy crossbows; call out the farmer-soldiers of the frontier colonies, add Wusun horse, and march straight on his fort. If he runs he has no refuge; if he stays he cannot hold the walls—a deed for the ages can be finished in a single dawn.

sentences.zh: 延壽亦以為然，欲奏請之，湯曰：「國家與公卿議，大策非凡所見，事必不從。」

translations.literal: Yanshou also thought it so, wishing to memorialize and request it; Tang said: When the state deliberates with ministers, great plans are not what common eyes see; the affair surely will not be granted.
translations.idiomatic: Gan Yanshou agreed and wanted to memorialize the throne, but Chen Tang said, Court debate never favors bold strokes; they will turn us down.

sentences.zh: 延壽猶與不聽。

translations.literal: Yanshou still hesitated and did not heed.
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou still hung back.

sentences.zh: 會其久病，湯獨矯制發城郭諸國兵、車師戊己校尉屯田使士。

translations.literal: It happened he was long ill; Tang alone forged orders mobilizing the walled states' troops and the Jushi Wu and Ji colonels' garrison-field officers and soldiers.
translations.idiomatic: While Yanshou lay ill for a long time Chen Tang alone forged an edict, calling up the oasis states and the garrison troops of the Wu and Ji colonels at Jushi.

sentences.zh: 延壽聞之，驚起，欲止焉。

translations.literal: When Yanshou heard it, he started up in alarm, wishing to stop it.
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou sprang from his sickbed in alarm and tried to halt the levy.

sentences.zh: 湯怒，按劍叱延壽曰：「大眾已集會，豎子欲沮眾邪？

translations.literal: Tang angrily grasped his sword and shouted at Yanshou: The great host is already assembled—does this stripling wish to thwart the host?
translations.idiomatic: Chen Tang gripped his sword and roared, The host is mustered—will you break the army's heart now?

sentences.zh: 延壽遂從之，部勒行陳，益置揚威、白虎、合騎之校，漢兵，胡兵合四萬餘人，延壽、湯上疏自劾奏矯制，陳言兵狀。

translations.literal: Yanshou then followed him, arranging battle lines, additionally establishing Raise Awe, White Tiger, and Combined Cavalry colonels; Han troops and Hu troops together over forty thousand men; Yanshou and Tang submitted a memorial impeaching themselves for forging orders, stating the military situation.
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou yielded, drew up the ranks, added colonels titled Raise Awe, White Tiger, and Combined Cavalry, and put more than forty thousand Han and Hu under arms; both men then memorialized their own forgery of orders and described the disposition of the force.

sentences.zh: 即日引軍分行，別為六校，其三校從南道逾蔥嶺徑大宛，其三校都護自將，發溫宿國，從北道入赤谷，過烏孫，涉康居界，至闐池西。

translations.literal: That day they led the army in separate columns, dividing into six colonels; three colonels from the south road crossed the Congling and took the direct route through Dawan; three colonels the protector-general personally led, setting out from Wensu state, from the north road entered Red Valley, passed Wusun, crossed Kangju territory, reaching west of Yanche (Pamir) pool.
translations.idiomatic: That day they split into six columns: three crossed the Pamirs on the southern road through Ferghana, while three under the commanding general marched from Wensu, took the northern road through Chigu past Wusun, crossed Kangju, and closed on the western shore of Lake Yanche.

sentences.zh: 而康居副王抱闐將數千騎，寇赤谷城東，殺略大昆彌千餘人，驅畜產甚多，從後與漢軍相及，頗寇盜後重。

translations.literal: But Kangju vice-king Baotian led several thousand riders, raiding east of Red Valley city, killing and plundering over a thousand of the great kunmi's people, driving off very many livestock; following from behind he caught up with the Han army and quite raided the rear baggage.
translations.idiomatic: Kangju's vice-king Baotian then struck east of Chigu with several thousand horsemen, killed or captured more than a thousand of the great kunmi's subjects, drove off huge herds, and harried the Han baggage train from the rear.

sentences.zh: 湯縱胡兵擊之，殺四百六十人，得其所略民四百七十人，還付大昆彌，其馬、牛、羊以給軍食。

translations.literal: Tang unleashed Hu troops to strike them, killing four hundred sixty, obtaining their plundered people four hundred seventy, returning them to the great kunmi; the horses, cattle, and sheep were used to supply army food.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Tang sent Hu auxiliaries against them, killed four hundred sixty, recovered four hundred seventy captives for the great kunmi, and used the captured herds to feed the army.

sentences.zh: 又捕得抱闐貴人伊奴毒。

translations.literal: Again they captured Baotian noble Yinudu.
translations.idiomatic: They also took the Baotian noble Yinudu prisoner.

sentences.zh: 入康居東界，令軍不得為寇。

translations.literal: Entering Kangju's eastern border, he ordered the army not to act as raiders.
translations.idiomatic: Inside Kangju's eastern marches he forbade looting.

sentences.zh: 間呼其貴人屠墨見之，諭以威信，與飲盟遣去。

translations.literal: Secretly he summoned their noble Tumo to meet him, instructed him with awe and trust, drank covenant with him and sent him away.
translations.idiomatic: He secretly summoned the Kangju noble Tumo, won him with promises of Han favor, swore an oath over wine, and sent him back.

sentences.zh: 徑引行，未至單于城可六十里，止營。

translations.literal: Straight he led the march; not yet reaching the chanyu's city, about sixty li, he halted camp.
translations.idiomatic: They marched on until some sixty li short of Zhizhi's fort and pitched camp.

sentences.zh: 復捕得康居貴人貝色子男開牟以為導。

translations.literal: Again they captured Kangju noble Beisezi's son Kai Mou to use as guide.
translations.idiomatic: They seized the Kangju noble Beisezi's son Kai Mou and pressed him into service as a guide.

sentences.zh: 貝色子即屠墨母之弟，皆怨單于，由是具知郅支情。

translations.literal: Beisezi was Tumo's mother's younger brother; all resented the chanyu; thereby they fully knew Zhizhi's situation.
translations.idiomatic: Beisezi was Tumo's uncle on his mother's side; both hated Zhizhi and revealed everything about his dispositions.

sentences.zh: 明日引行，未至城三十里，止營。

translations.literal: Next day they led the march; not yet reaching the city thirty li, halted camp.
translations.idiomatic: Next day they advanced to within thirty li of the walls and camped again.

sentences.zh: 單于遣使問：「漢兵何以來？」

translations.literal: The chanyu sent envoys asking: Why have Han troops come?
translations.idiomatic: Zhizhi sent envoys to ask why the Han host had come.

sentences.zh: 應曰：「單于上書言居困厄，願歸計強漢，身入朝見。

translations.literal: They replied: The chanyu in a memorial said dwelling in hardship, wished to submit plans to the strong Han, personally enter and attend audience.
translations.idiomatic: They answered that Zhizhi had written he was in distress, wished to submit to the mighty Han, and would come in person to court.

sentences.zh: 天子哀閔單于棄大國，屈意康居，故使都護將軍來迎單于妻子，恐左右驚動，故未敢至城下。」

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven pitied the chanyu for abandoning a great state and bending his will in Kangju; therefore he sent the protector-general and generals to welcome the chanyu's wife and children; fearing to startle those about him, therefore did not dare come below the walls.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor pitied him for leaving his realm and living under Kangju's thumb, so he sent the protector-general and his generals to escort the royal family; they halted short of the walls so as not to alarm the camp.

sentences.zh: 使數往來相答報。

translations.literal: Envoys repeatedly went back and forth mutually answering.
translations.idiomatic: Envoys shuttled back and forth with these replies.

sentences.zh: 延壽、湯因讓之：「我為單于遠來，而至今無名王大人見將軍受事者，何單于忽大計，失客主之禮也！

translations.literal: Yanshou and Tang thereupon reproached them: We for the chanyu came from afar, yet to this day there is no famous king or great man who has come to see the general receive affairs—how does the chanyu neglect great plan and lose guest-host rites!
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou and Tang then protested: We have marched a vast distance for your sake, yet no noble of rank has come out to greet the commander—how can you ignore the courtesies due between host and guest?

sentences.zh: 兵來道遠，人畜罷極，食度日盡，恐無以自還，願單于與大臣審計策。」

translations.literal: Troops came by a far road; men and beasts are exhausted extreme; food reckoning daily exhausted; fear there is no means to return ourselves; we beg the chanyu with great ministers carefully plan policy.
translations.idiomatic: Our men and beasts are spent, our rations nearly gone, and we may not get home unless you and your ministers settle on a course at once.

sentences.zh: 明日，前至郅支城都賴水上，離城三里，止營傅陳。

translations.literal: Next day they advanced to Zhizhi's city on the Talas water, three li from the city, halted camp and spread formation.
translations.idiomatic: The next day they reached Zhizhi's fort on the Talas River, halted three li out, and drew up for battle.

sentences.zh: 望見單于城上立五采幡幟，數百人披甲乘城，又出百餘騎往來馳城下，步兵百餘人夾門魚鱗陳，講習用兵。

translations.literal: They saw on the chanyu's walls multicolored banners raised, several hundred men in armor mounting the walls; also over a hundred riders came out galloping back and forth below the walls; over a hundred foot soldiers in fish-scale formation flanking the gate, drilling in the use of arms.
translations.idiomatic: They saw bright banners on the walls, hundreds of armored men manning the parapet, more than a hundred horsemen wheeling below the gate, and over a hundred foot in scale armor drilling in phalanx before the portal.

sentences.zh: 城上人更招漢軍曰「斗來！」

translations.literal: Those on the wall again beckoned the Han army saying: Come fight!
translations.idiomatic: Defenders on the wall shouted challenges for the Han to come fight.

sentences.zh: 百餘騎馳赴營，營皆張弩持滿指之，騎引卻。

translations.literal: Over a hundred riders galloped toward the camp; the camps all drew crossbows full and aimed at them; the riders drew back.
translations.idiomatic: More than a hundred riders charged the Han lines but pulled back when every camp raised loaded crossbows.

sentences.zh: 頗遣吏士射城門騎步兵，騎步兵皆入。

translations.literal: Quite sending clerks and soldiers to shoot the gate's cavalry and infantry, the cavalry and infantry all entered.
translations.idiomatic: Han archers shot at the enemy horse and foot by the gate until they withdrew inside.

sentences.zh: 延壽、湯令軍聞鼓音皆薄城下，四周圍城，各有所守，穿塹，塞門戶，鹵楯為前，戟弩為後，卬射城中樓上人，樓上人下走。

translations.literal: Yanshou and Tang ordered the army on hearing drum sound all press to below the walls, on four sides surround the city, each having its guard, dig moats, block gates and doors, shield-wall in front, halberd and crossbow behind, upward shoot those on the towers in the city; those on the towers fled down.
translations.idiomatic: At the drum Yanshou and Tang sent every detachment to the foot of the wall, ringed the fort, dug trenches, blocked the gates, put shieldmen in front and halberdiers and crossbowmen behind, and shot upward at the tower guards until they fled downstairs.

sentences.zh: 土城外有重木城，從木城中射，頗殺傷外人。

translations.literal: Outside the earthen wall was a double wooden wall; from within the wooden wall they shot, quite killing and wounding those outside.
translations.idiomatic: Outside the mud wall stood a palisade from whose loopholes the defenders shot and took a heavy toll of the besiegers.

sentences.zh: 外人發薪燒木城。

translations.literal: Those outside piled fuel and burned the wooden wall.
translations.idiomatic: The attackers stacked brush and burned the palisade.

sentences.zh: 夜，數百騎欲出外，迎射殺之。

translations.literal: At night several hundred riders wished to go out; they were met and shot and killed.
translations.idiomatic: That night several hundred horsemen tried to break out and were shot down as they emerged.

sentences.zh: 初，單于聞漢兵至，欲去，疑康居怨己，為漢內應，又聞烏孫諸國兵皆發，自以無所之。

translations.literal: At first when the chanyu heard Han troops had arrived, he wished to leave, suspected Kangju resented himself and would act as Han inner agents, also heard Wusun and various states' troops had all mobilized, himself thought there was nowhere to go.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhizhi first heard the Han were coming he meant to flee, but feared Kangju might turn on him as a Han ally and that Wusun and other states had mobilized—he saw no refuge.

sentences.zh: 郅支已出，復還，曰：「不如堅守。

translations.literal: Zhizhi had already gone out, returned again, saying: Better firmly defend.
translations.idiomatic: Zhizhi rode out, then turned back, saying, Better stand siege.

sentences.zh: 漢兵遠來，不能久攻。」

translations.literal: Han troops came from afar; they cannot long attack.
translations.idiomatic: The Han have marched too far to keep this up.

sentences.zh: 單于乃被甲在樓上，諸閼氏夫人數十皆以弓射外人。

translations.literal: The chanyu then donned armor on the tower; several tens of yanzhi ladies all with bows shot those outside.
translations.idiomatic: Zhizhi donned mail and stood on the tower while dozens of his wives shot bows at the attackers.

sentences.zh: 外人射中單于鼻，諸夫人頗死。

translations.literal: Those outside shot and hit the chanyu's nose; several ladies died.
translations.idiomatic: A Han shaft struck Zhizhi in the nose, and several of the women were killed.

sentences.zh: 單于下騎，傳戰大內。

translations.literal: The chanyu dismounted from horse, passed the fight into the great inner hall.
translations.idiomatic: Zhizhi left his horse and withdrew to fight inside the inner citadel.

sentences.zh: 夜過半，木城穿，中人卻入土城，乘城呼。

translations.literal: Past midnight the wooden wall was pierced; those within drew back into the earthen wall, mounted the wall and shouted.
translations.idiomatic: After midnight the palisade gave way; the defenders fell back into the mud wall and shouted from the parapet.

sentences.zh: 時，康居兵萬餘騎分為十餘處，四面環城，亦與相應和。

translations.literal: At the time Kangju troops over ten thousand riders divided into over ten places, on four sides ringed the city, also with them responded in concert.
translations.idiomatic: More than ten thousand Kangju horsemen in a dozen detachments ringed the city and tried to coordinate with the defenders.

sentences.zh: 夜，數奔營，不利，輒卻。

translations.literal: At night they several times charged the camp; not favorable, then drew back.
translations.idiomatic: They raided the Han camp several times that night but fell back each time without gain.

sentences.zh: 平明，四面火起，吏士喜，大呼乘之，鉦鼓聲動地。

translations.literal: At dawn fire rose on four sides; clerks and soldiers rejoiced, greatly shouting and riding upon it; gongs and drums shook the earth.
translations.idiomatic: At dawn fires blazed on every side; the Han troops roared and pressed the assault while gongs and drums shook the ground.

sentences.zh: 康居兵引卻。

translations.literal: Kangju troops drew back.
translations.idiomatic: The Kangju horse drew off.

sentences.zh: 漢兵四面推鹵楯，並入土城中。

translations.literal: Han troops on four sides pushed shield-walls and together entered the earthen wall city.
translations.idiomatic: Han shield walls closed in from every side and broke into the inner rampart.

sentences.zh: 單于男女百餘人走入大內。

translations.literal: The chanyu's men and women over a hundred persons ran into the great inner hall.
translations.idiomatic: Zhizhi and more than a hundred of his household fled into the inner keep.

sentences.zh: 漢兵縱火，吏士爭入，單于被創死。

translations.literal: Han troops set fire; clerks and soldiers vied to enter; the chanyu was wounded and died.
translations.idiomatic: The Han set fires and stormed the keep; Zhizhi died of his wounds.

sentences.zh: 軍候假丞杜勳斬單于首，得漢使節二及谷吉等所繼帛書。

translations.literal: Army investigator acting assistant Du Xun beheaded the chanyu's head, obtained two Han envoy tallies and the silk documents Gu Ji and others had carried.
translations.idiomatic: Du Xun, acting assistant to an army investigator, struck off Zhizhi's head and recovered two Han tallies and the silk letters Gu Ji had carried.

sentences.zh: 諸鹵獲以畀得者。

translations.literal: All booty was given to those who obtained it.
translations.idiomatic: Spoils went to the men who captured them.

sentences.zh: 凡斬閼氏、太子、名王以下千五百一十八級，生虜百四十五人，降虜千餘人，賦予城郭諸國所發十五王。

translations.literal: In all beheaded yanzhi, heir apparent, famous kings down 1,518 ranks; living captives 145 persons; surrendered captives over a thousand persons; distributed to the walled states' fifteen kings who had been mobilized.
translations.idiomatic: They took 1,518 heads from the royal ladies, the heir, and the nobility, 145 prisoners alive, and more than a thousand surrenders, which they parceled out among the fifteen kings of the oasis states who had joined the expedition.

sentences.zh: 於是延壽、湯上疏曰：「臣聞天下之大義，當混為一，昔有康、虞，今有強漢。

translations.literal: Thereupon Yanshou and Tang submitted a memorial saying: Your servants have heard that the great righteousness of all under heaven ought to be merged as one; anciently there were Kang and Yu; now there is the strong Han.
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou and Tang then memorialized: The empire's highest principle is unity; antiquity had the sage-kings Yao and Shun, and today there is the mighty Han.

sentences.zh: 匈奴呼韓邪單于已稱北籓，唯郅支單于叛逆，未伏其辜，大夏之西，以為強漢不能臣也。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu Chanyu Hu Hanxie has already called himself northern fence; only Chanyu Zhizhi rebelled and has not submitted to his guilt; west of Bactria he thought the strong Han could not make him subject.
translations.idiomatic: Hu Hanxie has already acknowledged himself a northern vassal, but Zhizhi rebelled unpunished and west of the Pamirs imagined the Han could never bring him to heel.

sentences.zh: 郅支單于慘毒行於民，大惡通於天。

translations.literal: Chanyu Zhizhi's cruel poison was practiced on the people; great evil reached to heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Zhizhi's cruelty toward the people was a crime that cried to Heaven.

sentences.zh: 臣延壽、臣湯將義兵，行天誅，賴陛下神靈，陰陽並應，天氣精明，陷陳克敵，斬郅支首及名王以下。

translations.literal: Your servants Yanshou and Tang led righteous troops, executed heaven's punishment; relying on Your Majesty's numen, yin and yang together responded, heaven's qi clear and bright, smashed formations and overcame the enemy, beheaded Zhizhi's head and those of famous kings down.
translations.idiomatic: We led a righteous host in Heaven's chastisement; by Your Majesty's power yin and yang favored us, the weather held clear, we broke the enemy line, and struck off Zhizhi's head together with those of his nobles.

sentences.zh: 宜縣頭槁街蠻夷邸間，以示萬里，明犯強漢者，雖遠必誅。」

translations.literal: It is fitting to suspend the head between Gaojie Street and the barbarian lodges, to show ten thousand li, making clear that those who offend the strong Han, though far, must be executed.
translations.idiomatic: His head should hang between Gaojie Lane and the barbarian hostel so that for ten thousand li all may read the lesson: whoever offends mighty Han, however far, will be cut down.

sentences.zh: 事下有司。

translations.literal: The matter was sent down to the responsible offices.
translations.idiomatic: The memorial was referred to the ministries.

sentences.zh: 丞相匡衡、御史大夫繁延壽以為：「郅支及名王首更歷諸國，蠻夷莫不聞知。

translations.literal: Chancellor Kuang Heng and Imperial Counselor Fan Yanshou thought: Zhizhi's and the famous kings' heads have passed through various states; barbarians none have not heard and known.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Kuang Heng and Imperial Counselor Fan Yanshou argued that Zhizhi's head and those of his nobles had passed through many lands and every tribe already knew the tale.

sentences.zh: 《月令》春：『掩骼埋胔』之時，宜勿縣。」

translations.literal: The Monthly Ordinances in spring: the time of 'covering bones and burying flesh'—it is fitting not to suspend.
translations.idiomatic: The Monthly Ordinances say spring is the season to bury exposed bones; the heads should not be displayed.

sentences.zh: 車騎將軍許嘉、右將軍王商以為：「春秋夾谷之會，優施笑君，孔子誅之，方盛夏，首足異門而出。

translations.literal: General of chariots and cavalry Xu Jia and Right General Wang Shang thought: At the Spring and Autumn Jiagu meeting, You Shi laughed at the ruler; Confucius executed him; just in midsummer, head and feet went out by different gates.
translations.idiomatic: General of Chariots Xu Jia and Right General Wang Shang cited the Jiagu meeting when Confucius had the buffoon You Shi executed for mocking his lord—even in high summer the corpse was dragged out by head and feet through different gates.

sentences.zh: 宜縣十日乃埋之。」

translations.literal: It is fitting to suspend ten days then bury them.
translations.idiomatic: Display the heads ten days, then bury them.

sentences.zh: 有詔將軍議是。

translations.literal: There was an edict that the generals' opinion was right.
translations.idiomatic: An edict approved the generals' view.

sentences.zh: 初，中書令石顯嘗欲以姊妻延壽，延壽不取。

translations.literal: At first Palace Secretary Shi Xian once wished to give his elder sister in marriage to Yanshou; Yanshou did not take her.
translations.idiomatic: Palace Secretary Shi Xian had once offered his sister to Gan Yanshou in marriage, and Yanshou refused.

sentences.zh: 及丞相、御史亦惡其矯制，皆不與湯。

translations.literal: When the chancellor and imperial counselor also hated their forging orders, they all were not for Tang.
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor and imperial counselor likewise resented the forged orders and sided against Chen Tang.

sentences.zh: 湯素貪，所鹵獲財物入塞多不法。

translations.literal: Tang was by nature greedy; the plundered goods and wealth entering the passes were mostly irregular.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Tang was notoriously avaricious, and much of the loot he brought through the passes was irregularly accounted.

sentences.zh: 司隸校尉移書道上，系吏士按驗之。

translations.literal: The metropolitan commandant sent documents on the road, binding clerks and soldiers to investigate.
translations.idiomatic: The metropolitan commandant sent orders along the route to arrest officers and men for questioning.

sentences.zh: 湯上疏言：「臣與吏士共誅郅支單于，幸得禽滅，萬里振旅，宜有使者迎勞道路。

translations.literal: Tang submitted a memorial saying: Your servant with clerks and soldiers together executed Chanyu Zhizhi; fortunately obtained capture and destruction; ten thousand li shaking the army; it is fitting to have envoys welcome and console on the road.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Tang memorialized: We destroyed Zhizhi ten thousand li from home; envoys should greet us on the road with praise, not fetters.

sentences.zh: 今司隸反逆收系按驗，是為郅支報仇也！」

translations.literal: Now the metropolitan commandant contrarywise arrests and binds for investigation—this is avenging Zhizhi!
translations.idiomatic: To have the metropolitan commandant seize us instead is to avenge Zhizhi!

sentences.zh: 上立出吏士，令縣道具酒食以過軍。

translations.literal: The emperor immediately released clerks and soldiers, ordering counties along the route to prepare wine and food for the passing army.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor at once freed the men and ordered every county on their line of march to supply wine and food for the army.

sentences.zh: 既至，論功，石顯、匡衡以為：「延壽、湯擅興師矯制，幸得不誅，如復加爵土，則後奉使者爭欲乘危徼幸，生事於蠻夷，為國招難，漸不可開。」

translations.literal: When they arrived and merits were debated, Shi Xian and Kuang Heng thought: Yanshou and Tang on their own raised troops and forged orders; fortunately they were not executed; if moreover titles and land are added, then later mission envoys will all wish to ride danger seeking luck, raising affairs among barbarians, inviting difficulty for the state—a trend that cannot be opened.
translations.idiomatic: When rewards were debated Shi Xian and Kuang Heng argued that Yanshou and Tang had mobilized without orders and forged an edict; they had been spared execution, and to ennoble them now would teach every future envoy to court danger and stir trouble on the frontier—a precedent the state must not set.

sentences.zh: 元帝內嘉延壽、湯功，而重違衡、顯之議，議久不決。

translations.literal: Emperor Yuan inwardly praised Yanshou and Tang's merit, yet heavily went against Heng and Xian's deliberation; deliberation long was not decided.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Yuan admired their feat yet shrank from overruling Kuang Heng and Shi Xian, and the debate dragged on without decision.

sentences.zh: 故宗正劉向上疏曰：「郅支單于囚殺使者吏士以百數，事暴揚外國，傷威毀重，群臣皆閔焉。

translations.literal: Former Director of the Imperial Clan Liu Xiang submitted a memorial saying: Chanyu Zhizhi imprisoned and killed envoys and clerks and soldiers by the hundreds; the affair was exposed abroad, injuring awe and destroying weight; all ministers grieved.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xiang, former director of the imperial clan, memorialized: Zhizhi murdered hundreds of Han envoys and soldiers, shaming the dynasty before every foreign court, and the ministers grieved for it.

sentences.zh: 陛下赫然欲誅之，意未嘗有忘。

translations.literal: Your Majesty awesomely wished to execute him; the intent has never once been forgotten.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty's wrath against him has never slackened.

sentences.zh: 西域都護延壽、副校尉湯承聖指，倚神靈，總百蠻之君，攬城郭之兵，出百死，入絕域，遂蹈康居，屠五重城，搴歙侯之旗，斬郅支之首，縣旌萬里之外，揚威昆山之西，掃谷吉之恥，立昭明之功，萬夷懾伏，莫不懼震。

translations.literal: Western Regions Protector-general Yanshou and Vice Colonel Tang received sage commands, relied on numen, gathered the hundred barbarians' lords, grasped walled states' troops, went out from hundred deaths, entered extreme regions, then trod Kangju, slaughtered fivefold walls, plucked the She marquis's banner, beheaded Zhizhi's head, suspended banners beyond ten thousand li, raised awe west of Kunlun, swept away Gu Ji's shame, established brilliant-clear merit; myriad barbarians trembled and submitted; none did not fear and shake.
translations.idiomatic: Protector-general Yanshou and Vice Colonel Tang obeyed Your Majesty's command, leaned on Heaven's favor, rallied the chieftains of the west, led the oasis militias, crossed a hundred deaths into the void beyond the maps, stormed Kangju, tore through five lines of walls, tore down the Shehou's standard, struck off Zhizhi's head, and planted the Han banner beyond ten thousand li. West of the Kunlun they restored Han prestige, wiped away Gu Ji's disgrace, and struck such terror into every tribe that all trembled.

sentences.zh: 呼韓邪單于見郅支已誅，且喜且懼，鄉風馳義，稽首來賓，願守北籓，累世稱臣。

translations.literal: Chanyu Hu Hanxie seeing Zhizhi already executed, both rejoiced and feared, turned to the wind and galloped righteousness, knocked head and came as guest, wished to guard the northern fence, generation after generation calling himself subject.
translations.idiomatic: When Hu Hanxie saw Zhizhi dead he was both glad and afraid; he hurried to court, kowtowed as a guest, and offered to hold the northern marches as a vassal for generations.

sentences.zh: 立千載之功，建萬世之安，群臣大勳莫大焉。

translations.literal: They established a thousand ages' merit, built ten thousand generations' peace; among ministers' great deeds none is greater.
translations.idiomatic: They won a triumph for the ages and a peace for posterity; no minister's deed has been greater.

sentences.zh: 昔周大夫方叔、吉甫為宣王誅獫狁而百蠻從，其《詩》曰：「嘽□焞□，如霆如雷，顯允方叔，征伐獫狁，蠻荊來威。』

translations.literal: Formerly Zhou grandees Fang Shu and Jifu for King Xuan executed the Xianyun and the hundred barbarians followed; its Odes says: Tan tan, tun tun, like thunderbolt like thunder, illustrious and trusty Fang Shu, campaigns against Xianyun; Man of Jing come in awe.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhou's Fang Shu and Yin Jifu crushed the Xianyun for King Xuan, the Odes sang how their chariots rolled like thunder and Fang Shu's host awed the southern Man into submission.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰：『有嘉折首，獲匪其醜。』

translations.literal: The Changes says: There is praise for beheading the leader; captives are not their ugly followers.
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Changes says, Beheading the ringleader wins praise; the rest are not treated as common criminals.

sentences.zh: 言美誅首惡之人，而諸不順者皆來從也。

translations.literal: It means beautifully executing the chief evil man, and the various disobedient all come to follow.
translations.idiomatic: It means strike the chief culprit and every wavering band will submit.

sentences.zh: 今延壽、湯所誅震，雖《易》之折首、《詩》之雷霆不能及也。

translations.literal: Now the awe Yanshou and Tang struck exceeds even the Changes' beheading leader and the Odes' thunderbolt.
translations.idiomatic: The terror Yanshou and Tang have inspired goes beyond the Book of Changes' counsel or the thunder of the Odes.

sentences.zh: 論大功者不錄小過，舉大美者不疵細瑕。

translations.literal: Discussing great merit one does not record small faults; praising great beauty one does not fault fine flaws.
translations.idiomatic: Great deeds forgive small lapses, and great beauty is not marred by a speck.

sentences.zh: 《司馬法》曰『軍賞不逾月』，欲民速得為善之利也。

translations.literal: The Sima Methods says 'military rewards do not pass the month,' wishing the people quickly to obtain the profit of doing good.
translations.idiomatic: The Sima Methods says military rewards must be paid within the month so the people quickly taste the fruit of virtue.

sentences.zh: 蓋急武功，重用人也。

translations.literal: This is stressing military merit and valuing the use of men.
translations.idiomatic: That is how a state stresses martial success and honors its fighting men.

sentences.zh: 吉甫之歸，周厚賜之，其《詩》曰：『吉甫燕喜，既多受祉，來歸自鎬，我行永久。』

translations.literal: When Jifu returned, Zhou richly rewarded him; its Odes says: Jifu feasts in joy, having received many blessings; coming back from Hao, my march was long enduring.
translations.idiomatic: When Jifu came home the Zhou king heaped gifts on him, and the Odes record his feast of joy, the many blessings he received, and how long the road from Hao had felt.

sentences.zh: 千里之鎬猶以為遠，況萬里之外，其勤至矣！

translations.literal: A thousand li to Hao was still thought far; how much more beyond ten thousand li—their toil reached the utmost!
translations.idiomatic: A thousand li to Hao seemed long—what of a march ten thousand li? Their labor was extreme.

sentences.zh: 延壽、湯既未獲受祉之報，反屈捐命之功，久挫於刀筆之前，非所以勸有功厲戎士也。

translations.literal: Yanshou and Tang having not yet obtained the reward of receiving blessings, on the contrary humiliate the merit of risking life, long thwarted before knife-and-brush clerks—this is not the means to encourage those with merit and sharpen martial soldiers.
translations.idiomatic: Instead of blessings they face clerks' quibbles—hardly the way to reward men who risked their lives or to steel the army's heart.

sentences.zh: 昔齊桓公前有尊周之功，後有滅項之罪；

translations.literal: Formerly Duke Huan of Qi before had the merit of honoring Zhou, afterward had the crime of destroying Xiang;
translations.idiomatic: Duke Huan of Qi first honored the Zhou house, then wiped out Xiang—

sentences.zh: 君子以功覆過而為之諱行事。

translations.literal: the gentleman by merit covers fault and for him conceals the deed.
translations.idiomatic: yet gentlemen let his merit bury the fault and pass over the deed in silence.

sentences.zh: 貳師將軍李廣利捐五萬之師，靡億萬之費，經四年這勞，而廑獲駿馬三十匹，雖斬宛王毋鼓之首，猶不足以復費，其私罪惡甚多。

translations.literal: Second Division General Li Guangli threw away fifty thousand troops, exhausted hundred-million expense, passed four years of this toil, yet only obtained thirty fine horses; although beheading Ferghana king Wugu's head, still was insufficient to repay the expense; his private crimes and evils were very many.
translations.idiomatic: General Li Guangli of the Second Division wasted fifty thousand men and untold treasure over four years of campaigning for thirty horses; even beheading King Wugu of Ferghana did not repay the cost, and his private crimes were many.

sentences.zh: 孝武以為萬里征伐，不錄其過，遂封拜兩侯、三卿、二千石百有餘人。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaowu thought that for ten-thousand-li campaigns he would not record their faults; thereupon he enfeoffed and appointed two marquises, three ministers, and over a hundred officials at two thousand piculs.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu held that a ten-thousand-li war excuses much, and ennobled two marquises, three ministers, and more than a hundred grandees at two thousand piculs despite the cost.

sentences.zh: 今康居國強於大宛，郅支之號重於宛王，殺使者罪甚於留馬，而延壽、湯不煩漢士，不費斗糧，比於貳師，功德百之。

translations.literal: Now Kangju state is stronger than Dawan; Zhizhi's title is weightier than the Ferghana king's; killing envoys' crime is graver than detaining horses; yet Yanshou and Tang did not trouble Han soldiers, did not cost a peck of grain—compared to the Second Division, their merit and virtue are a hundredfold.
translations.idiomatic: Kangju is stronger than Ferghana was, Zhizhi's title outranks Wugu's, and murdering envoys is a worse offense than holding horses—yet Yanshou and Tang did it without drafting interior troops or spending a peck of state grain; beside Li Guangli their merit is a hundredfold.

sentences.zh: 且常惠隨欲擊之烏孫，鄭吉迎自來之日逐，猶皆裂土受爵。

translations.literal: Moreover Chang Hui as he wished struck Wusun; Zheng Ji welcomed the Rizhi who came of himself—still both were granted fiefs and received nobility.
translations.idiomatic: Chang Hui's raid on Wusun and Zheng Ji's reception of the Rizhi king, far slighter feats, both won fiefs and titles.

sentences.zh: 故言威武勤勞則大於方叔、吉甫，列功覆過則優於齊桓、貳師，近事之功則高於安遠、長羅，而大功未著，小惡數布，臣竊痛之！

translations.literal: Therefore speaking of martial awe and toil they are greater than Fang Shu and Jifu; arraying merit to cover fault they excel Duke Huan of Qi and the Second Division; in recent affairs' merit they are higher than Anyuan and Changluo—yet great merit is not yet displayed while small evils are repeatedly spread; your servant privately grieves!
translations.idiomatic: In martial toil they surpass Fang Shu and Jifu; in letting merit cover fault they outdo Duke Huan and Li Guangli; among recent deeds they tower above Zheng Ji and Chang Hui—yet their great deed goes unrewarded while petty faults are trumpeted abroad; your servant grieves for it!

sentences.zh: 宜以時解縣通籍，除過勿治，尊寵爵位，以勸有功。」

translations.literal: It is fitting in due time release from suspension and open the registers, remove faults and not prosecute, honor and favor rank and position, in order to encourage those with merit.
translations.idiomatic: Dismiss the charges, restore their names, pardon their faults, and raise them in rank to encourage merit.

sentences.zh: 於是天子下詔曰：「匈奴郅支單于背畔禮義，留殺漢使者、吏士，甚逆道理，朕豈忘之哉！

translations.literal: Thereupon the Son of Heaven issued an edict saying: The Xiongnu Chanyu Zhizhi rebelled against ritual and right, detained and killed Han envoys and clerks and soldiers—greatly opposing principle and pattern; could We forget it!
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then decreed: Zhizhi of the Xiongnu broke faith, murdered Han envoys and soldiers—how could We forget?

sentences.zh: 所以優遊而不征者，重協師眾，勞將帥，故隱忍而未有雲也。

translations.literal: The reason We leisurely did not punish was weighing coordinated hosts and masses, toiling generals and commanders; therefore We bore it in silence and had not yet moved.
translations.idiomatic: We held back from punishing because a campaign would strain the hosts and weary the generals, and so bore the insult in patience.

sentences.zh: 今延壽、湯睹便宜，乘時利，結城郭諸國，擅興師矯制而征之。

translations.literal: Now Yanshou and Tang saw convenience, rode the season's profit, knotted the walled states, on their own raised troops and forged orders to punish them.
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou and Tang seized the moment, rallied the oasis states, forged an edict, and marched against him on their own authority.

sentences.zh: 賴天地宗廟之靈，誅討郅支單于，斬獲其首，及閼氏、貴人、名王以下千數。

translations.literal: Relying on Heaven and Earth and the ancestral temples' numen, they punished and attacked Chanyu Zhizhi, beheaded and captured his head, and yanzhi, nobles, famous kings down by the thousands.
translations.idiomatic: By favor of Heaven, Earth, and the imperial ancestors they struck Zhizhi down, took his head, and slew or captured thousands among his wives, nobles, and chieftains.

sentences.zh: 雖逾義干法，內不煩一夫之役，不開府庫之臧，因敵之糧以贍軍用，立功萬里之外，威震百蠻，名顯四海。

translations.literal: Although exceeding righteousness and offending law, within they did not trouble one man's corvée, did not open the treasury's stores, relied on the enemy's grain to supply military use, established merit beyond ten thousand li, awe shook the hundred barbarians, name was manifest within the four seas.
translations.idiomatic: Though they overstepped law and precedent, they did not levy a single laborer from the interior or open the treasuries—they fed the army from the enemy—and won glory ten thousand li away that awed every tribe and resounded across the realm.

sentences.zh: 為國除殘，兵革之原息，邊竟得以安。

translations.literal: For the state they removed the cruel; the root of arms and leather ceased; the border frontier thereby obtained peace.
translations.idiomatic: They rid the empire of a scourge, stilled the springs of war, and brought quiet to the frontier.

sentences.zh: 然猶不免死亡之患，罪當在於奉憲，朕甚閔之！

translations.literal: Yet still they did not avoid the peril of death and punishment; guilt ought to lie in observing the statutes—We greatly pity them!
translations.idiomatic: Yet they still face the headsman's law for breaking regulations—We pity them deeply.

sentences.zh: 其赦延壽、湯罪，勿治。

translations.literal: Let Yanshou and Tang's crimes be pardoned; do not prosecute.
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou and Tang are pardoned and shall not be prosecuted.

sentences.zh: 詔公卿議封焉。

translations.literal: An edict ordered ministers to deliberate enfeoffment.
translations.idiomatic: The court was ordered to debate their rewards.

sentences.zh: 議者皆以為宜如軍法捕斬單于令。

translations.literal: The debaters all thought it fitting to follow the military law on capturing and beheading a chanyu.
translations.idiomatic: Most officials thought the bounty prescribed for killing a chanyu should apply.

sentences.zh: 匡衡、石顯以為「郅支本亡逃失國，竊號絕域，非真單于」。

translations.literal: Kuang Heng and Shi Xian thought: Zhizhi originally was a fugitive who lost his state, usurped title in extreme regions—not a true chanyu.
translations.idiomatic: Kuang Heng and Shi Xian argued that Zhizhi was a runaway pretender in the wilds, not a true chanyu.

sentences.zh: 元帝取安遠侯鄭吉故事，封千戶，衡、顯復爭。

translations.literal: Emperor Yuan took the precedent of Marquis of Anyuan Zheng Ji, enfeoffing one thousand households; Heng and Xian again disputed.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Yuan proposed a thousand households after the precedent of Marquis Zheng Ji; Kuang Heng and Shi Xian objected again.

sentences.zh: 乃封延壽為義成侯。

translations.literal: Thereupon Yanshou was enfeoffed as marquis of Yicheng.
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou was therefore enfeoffed as Marquis of Yicheng.

sentences.zh: 賜湯爵關內侯，食邑各三百戶，加賜黃金百斤。

translations.literal: Tang was granted rank as marquis within the passes, revenue of three hundred households, additionally granted one hundred jin of gold.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Tang received the rank of marquis within the passes with three hundred households, plus a hundred jin of gold.

sentences.zh: 告上帝、宗廟，大赦天下。

translations.literal: They reported to High God and the ancestral temple and issued a great amnesty for all under heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Victory was reported to Heaven and the ancestors, and a general amnesty was proclaimed.

sentences.zh: 拜延壽為長水校尉，湯為射聲校尉。

translations.literal: Yanshou was appointed colonel of the Changshui archers; Tang was appointed colonel of the Shedding Sound corps.
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou was named colonel of the Changshui archers and Chen Tang colonel of the Shedding Sound corps.

sentences.zh: 延壽遷城門校尉、護軍都尉，薨於官。

translations.literal: Yanshou was promoted colonel of the gates and colonel-protector of the army; he died in office.
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou rose to colonel of the gates and colonel-protector of the army and died in harness.

sentences.zh: 成帝初即位，丞相衡復奏：「湯以吏二千石奉使，顓命蠻夷中，不正身以先下，而盜所收康居財物，戒官屬曰絕域事不復校。

translations.literal: When Emperor Cheng had just ascended, Chancellor Heng again memorialized: Tang as an official at two thousand piculs carried mission, monopolized command among barbarians, did not correct his person to lead those below, but stole confiscated Kangju goods, warning subordinates that affairs beyond the pale would not again be checked.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Cheng's reign Chancellor Kuang Heng memorialized again that Chen Tang, a grandee at two thousand piculs on embassy among the tribes, had failed to set an example, embezzled Kangju property from the seizures, and told his staff that no one would audit deeds beyond the frontier.

sentences.zh: 雖在赦前，不宜處位。」

translations.literal: Although before the amnesty, it is not fitting he hold office.
translations.idiomatic: Even though the acts preceded the amnesty, he should not keep his post.

sentences.zh: 湯坐免。

translations.literal: Tang sat crime and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Tang was dismissed for his offenses.

sentences.zh: 後湯上書言康居王侍子非王子也。

translations.literal: Later Tang submitted a memorial saying the Kangju king's attendant son was not a true prince.
translations.idiomatic: Later Chen Tang memorialized that the Kangju prince at court was an impostor.

sentences.zh: 按驗，實王子也。

translations.literal: On investigation he was indeed a prince.
translations.idiomatic: Investigation proved him a genuine prince.

sentences.zh: 湯下獄當死。

translations.literal: Tang was cast into prison and liable to death.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Tang was jailed and faced execution.

sentences.zh: 太中大夫谷永上疏訟湯曰：「臣聞楚有子玉得臣，文公為之仄席而坐；
